title: Malaysian Garlic Butter Prawns

ingredients.name: large prawn, shell on (deveined through the shell)
visual_checks: shell-on prawns absorb the butter better and taste richer
ingredients.notes: shell-on prawns absorb the butter better and taste richer

subs.name: 500 g fish fillets cut in 4 cm pieces
subs.notes: reduce initial sear to 1 minute per side to avoid overcooking

subs.name: ghee for a rich Indian-kitchen version
subs.notes: ghee handles high heat better than butter and gives a more Indian flavour bridge

ingredients.name: dried red chillies, broken in half
visual_checks: adjust for heat tolerance; remove seeds for less heat
ingredients.notes: adjust for heat tolerance; remove seeds for less heat

subs.name: 0.5 tsp red chilli flakes or 1 tsp sambal oelek (chilli sauce)

ingredients.name: spring onion, cut in 3 cm pieces
oyster sauce
visual_checks: adds the savoury depth that makes the sauce glossy
ingredients.notes: adds the savoury depth that makes the sauce glossy

subs.name: 1 tbsp hoisin sauce or 1 tsp extra soy sauce with a pinch of sugar
subs.notes: hoisin gives sweetness and body like oyster sauce

steps.text: Pat the prawns dry with kitchen paper — moisture will cause the butter to splatter. Mix oyster sauce, soy sauce, lemon juice and sugar in a small bowl and set aside.

Heat oil in a wide frying pan or tawa on high. Add the prawns in a single layer and sear without moving for 1.5 minutes until the underside is pink and a little charred. Flip and sear the other side for 1 minute. Remove and set aside.

Reduce heat to medium. Add butter and let it melt and foam. Add garlic and dried red chillies and fry, stirring constantly, for 1.5–2 minutes until the garlic turns pale golden and smells nutty. Do not let the garlic brown — it turns bitter quickly in butter.
Pour in the sauce mixture and stir. The butter sauce will hiss and bubble — let it simmer for 30 seconds until slightly thickened.

Return the prawns to the pan. Toss to coat every prawn in the garlic butter sauce, then add the spring onion pieces. Cook on medium heat for 1 more minute, turning the prawns, until everything is glossy and the prawns are cooked through.
Taste for salt — the oyster and soy sauces bring significant saltiness. Serve immediately over steamed jasmine rice with extra lime wedges on the side. Eat the prawns shell-on for maximum flavour.

cross_contact_notes: Ingredient metadata cannot prove an allergen-free kitchen. Check packaged labels and cross-contact statements for your products.
safety_warnings: Keep raw animal products, their plates and utensils separate from cooked food; wash hands and surfaces after contact.
critical_checks: Cook seafood until safely done for the species and preparation; keep it chilled before cooking.
trust.provenance_summary: AI-assisted original draft; declared licence original.
trust.substitution_warning: A substitution can introduce allergens or change dietary suitability. Never claim a swap is safe without checking the replacement's label and the user's allergy context.

substitution_notes: butter → ghee for a rich Indian-kitchen version (ghee handles high heat better than butter and gives a more Indian flavour bridge); prawn → 500 g fish fillets cut in 4 cm pieces (reduce initial sear to 1 minute per side to avoid overcooking); oyster-sauce → 1 tbsp hoisin sauce or 1 tsp extra soy sauce with a pinch of sugar (hoisin gives sweetness and body like oyster sauce); dried-red-chilli → 0.5 tsp red chilli flakes or 1 tsp sambal oelek (chilli sauce) (easier to control the heat)
indian_kitchen_adaptation: Use a heavy tawa or kadai on the highest flame. Ghee can substitute for butter and is even better for high-heat cooking. Fresh prawns (eral/jhinga) from the local market work perfectly. Oyster sauce is available at large supermarkets; replace it with a teaspoon of extra soy sauce and a pinch of sugar if unavailable. Dried red chillies (byadgi or Kashmiri) are standard Indian pantry items. This dish works with tail-on king prawns found at any seafood market.
